topic: new office carpet — choosing between two Persian rug vendors

turns.content: I need your opinion on something, jan. I am replacing the carpet in my practice's waiting room, and I have narrowed it down to two Persian rug vendors. But I cannot decide and it is consuming an unreasonable amount of my attention.
I'd be happy to help you think through it. What are the two options? Tell me about each vendor and what they're offering — style, quality, price range, anything that's making the decision difficult.

The first vendor is a shop in La Jolla that specializes in antique Tabriz rugs. Beautiful pieces, hand-knotted, very fine detail. The owner is an older gentleman from Isfahan who knows his craft. He has a piece that is approximately twelve by fifteen feet, a traditional medallion pattern in navy and ivory. It is stunning. But it is $8,200.
That sounds like a genuinely museum-quality piece. Tabriz rugs with fine medallion patterns in those colors are timeless. What's the second option?

The second vendor is online — they source new production rugs from workshops in Iran. Very high quality but not antique. They have a Kashan-style piece in similar dimensions, a rich crimson and gold palette, dense weave. It is $3,400. Objectively beautiful, and frankly more practical for a waiting room where patients will be walking across it daily.

So you're really weighing artistry and provenance against practicality and value. The Tabriz from the La Jolla shop is a statement piece — it tells patients something about you before you even walk into the room. The Kashan-style piece is smart money and durable. What's the current waiting room aesthetic like? That might help decide which one fits better.

The waiting room is modern and clean. Neutral tones, good lighting, comfortable seating. I designed it to feel calm and professional — not sterile, but not cluttered either. The rug would be the focal point. That is why I keep going back to the Tabriz. It has character. It has a story. When a patient sits down, I want them to feel that they are in a place where someone cares about details.

You just answered your own question, I think. You described wanting a piece with character and story that communicates attention to detail — that's the Tabriz. In a neutral, modern space, an antique Persian rug becomes the soul of the room. The Kashan would look fine, but "fine" doesn't sound like your standard. The $4,800 difference is real, but for a piece you and your patients will see every day for years, it might be the right investment. My only practical question: can an antique like that handle foot traffic, or would you need to position it away from the main walkway?

That is exactly the concern Shirin raised. She said, 'Dariush, you are going to put an antique rug where people in wet shoes will step on it and then you will lose sleep.' She is not wrong. The vendor in La Jolla said that a good Tabriz can handle moderate traffic if properly padded underneath and cleaned professionally once a year. But Shirin knows me — I will notice every scuff.

Shirin knows you well. If you're the kind of person who will notice every scuff — and from what you've described, you absolutely are — then you need to factor in whether the Tabriz will bring you joy or anxiety once it's on the floor of a working medical office. One option: put the Tabriz in your private office or consultation room where traffic is controlled and you can enjoy it without the stress. Then use the Kashan for the waiting room where it will absorb the daily wear gracefully. You get both the artistry and the practicality.

That is actually a very elegant solution, jan. The Tabriz in my consultation room where I sit with patients one on one — they would see it and it sets the tone. The Kashan handles the waiting room. Shirin will approve of this compromise. And my consultation room has lower traffic, so the antique stays pristine.

That's the best of both worlds. The consultation room is where you build trust with patients face to face, so having a piece with genuine heritage there reinforces exactly the impression you want. And the Kashan in the waiting room gives warmth and beauty without keeping you up at night worrying about shoe prints. It does mean spending on both, but the total is still reasonable for what you're getting. Would you go back to the La Jolla shop in person to finalize the Tabriz?

I will go this Saturday. I want to see it one more time in person before I commit. And I will order the Kashan online this evening — at $3,400 it is straightforward. Thank you for helping me think through this, jan. I was going in circles. Shirin will say you have more sense than I do, and she would be right.

Happy to help untangle it. When you visit the La Jolla shop Saturday, ask about the rug's specific origin city and approximate age — pieces like that often come with documentation or at least an oral history. It's the kind of detail that makes a great story when a patient admires it in your consultation room. Enjoy the process — decorating a space you've built yourself should be a pleasure, not a headache.
